statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical diesel reserves and widespread shortfalls across all fuel types following recent strategic reserve releases.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure as strategic reserve releases to counter Middle East supply disruptions have left most EU countries below mandatory minimums. While EU averages show petrol at 96.3 days and diesel at 88.8 days—both near the 90-day threshold—the distribution is highly uneven. A striking 19 of 27 countries now hold critical diesel reserves, with Romania's diesel stocks at just 30.2 days representing the most severe shortfall.

Jet fuel reserves present the most acute concern, averaging only 87.3 days across the EU with 17 of 27 countries in critical status. Poland's jet fuel stocks at 22.5 days and Portugal's at 21.2 days highlight the severity of aviation fuel shortages. This pattern reflects the sector's particular vulnerability to Middle East supply chain disruptions and limited alternative sourcing options.

Oil prices have surged 26% since the late-February escalation began, with Brent crude reaching $91.36 per barrel despite today's 3% decline. This price pressure, combined with depleted strategic reserves, creates a challenging environment for rebuilding stocks. Baltic states Estonia and Latvia maintain relatively strong positions, while Central European countries including Poland, Austria, and Romania face the most severe combined shortfalls across multiple fuel types.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries hold critical diesel reserves below the 90-day mandatory minimum, with Romania at just 30.2 days
Jet fuel shortage affects 17 of 27 countries critically, averaging 87.3 days EU-wide with Poland and Portugal below 23 days
Only 8 of 27 countries maintain adequate petrol reserves above 90 days, despite EU average of 96.3 days
Brent crude oil has risen 26% since late February to $91.36/barrel, increasing costs for reserve replenishment
Central European countries face the most severe multi-fuel shortages, while Baltic states maintain stronger positions
